rulings.Case Title: CHARLES LI VS. DEMAS YAN et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Notice Of Motion To Compel Compliance With Order, To Compel Post-Judgment Discovery Answers, And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON TUESDAY, OCTOBER 25, 2016, LINE 5 PLAINTIFF CHARLES LI'S Motion To Compel Compliance With Order, To Compel Post-Judgment Discovery Answers, And For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Stephanie Krmpotic, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted. No opposition filed. Defendant is to produce documents in compliance with the April 16, 2015 Order re Subpoena Duces Tecum by October 31, 2016. Defendant shall fully respond to Post-Judgment Interrogatories by November 9, 2016. All objections have been waived. Defendant shall produce all documents responsive to Plaintiff's Post-Judgment Request For Production, Set Two or provide a statement under oath by November 9, 2016. All responses are to be verified. Sanctions awarded to plaintiff in the amount of $2,500. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to JSKrmpotic@LOWBALL.COM with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/JPT

rulings.Case Title: THOMAS D. ROTH VS. SECURITY NATIONAL GUARANTY, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioners Thomas Roth And Blum Collins Llp'S Motion And Petition For Court To Appoint Arbitrator Or Follow Procedures In Code Of Civil Procedure Section 1281.6
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 25, 2016, Line 16, Petitioners Thomas Roth And Blum Collins LLP's Motion And Petition For Court To Appoint Arbitrator Or Follow Procedures In Code Of Civil Procedure Section 1281.6. Hearing required unless by 4pm on October 24, 2016, counsel for one or both sides notifies the court by email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all parties that the parties have selected an arbitrator. If they have done so, this motion will go off calendar. If they have not done so, at the hearing the court will appoint the arbitrator from among the five nominees. There will be a single arbitrator to resolve all arbitrable issues raised by the parties. The arbitrator has the authority to divide or bifurcate the issues by petitioner or in any other way or not to do so. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: KWIK BOND POLYMERS, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIAB VS. CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Motion For Summary Adjudication; Notice Of Motion
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 25, 2016, Line 2, PLAINTIFF KWIK BOND POLYMERS, LLC, MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Motion For Summary Adjudication. This motion was argued on October 13, 2016 and the hearing was continued to October 25, 2016 for the sole purpose of issuance of the court's ruling. After review of the additional papers filed by the parties concerning the legislative history of Public Contract Code 10225, the court believes that section 10225 does not impose any mandatory duty on CalTrans after a payment bond has been filed that complies with the requirements for such a bond. Accordingly, the court now adopts the tentative ruling issued in advance of the October 13 hearing: Plaintiff Kwik Bond Polymers, LLC's motion for summary judgment and alternate motion for summary adjudication are denied. Kwik Bond has not satisfied its initial summary judgment burden. Kwik Bond cannot show Caltrans breached any mandatory duty imposed by statute. Kwik Bond does not cite any authority imposing a mandatory duty requiring Caltrans to halt payments to general contractor Victory Engineers, Inc. or to obtain a replacement bond in the event that the surety was in liquidation proceedings. Notwithstanding the contrary ruling by Judge Goldsmith on Caltrans' demurrer to the second amended complaint, there is no mandatory duty imposed by any of the statutes cited in the second amended complaint or in Kwik Bonds' papers on this motion that requires Caltrans to ensure the efficacy of a payment bond after the payment bond once a payment bond has been filed that complies with the requirements for such a bond. The undisputed facts establish that Caltrans ensured that a statutorily compliant payment bond was filed as to each of the public work contracts at issue in this case prior to the execution of the contract for each of the public work projects. Having done so, Caltrans discharged the sole mandatory duty imposed on it with regard to the securing of a payment bond as to each public work project. There is no California authority that imposed any further duty on Caltrans such as a duty to ensure the efficacy of the payment bond in light of the financial, legal or other circumstances of the general contractor who filed the bond or the surety who issued the bond. Public Contract Code 10225 does not impose such a duty and, even if it did, that duty would be limited to circumstances that do not obtain here where the surety cancelled or withdrew a payment bond. Because there is no mandatory duty as alleged in the second amended complaint, Caltrans is entitled to judgment in its favor. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: KWIK BOND POLYMERS, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIAB VS. CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 25, 2016, Line 2, DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Defendant California Department of Transportation's motion for summary judgment is granted. This motion was argued on October 13, 2016 and the hearing was continued to October 25, 2016 for the sole purpose of issuance of the court's ruling. After review of the additional papers filed by the parties concerning the legislative history of Public Contract Code 10225, the court believes that section 10225 does not impose any mandatory duty on CalTrans after a payment bond has been filed that complies with the requirements for such a bond. Accordingly, the court now adopts the tentative ruling issued in advance of the October 13 hearing: Notwithstanding the contrary ruling by Judge Goldsmith on Caltrans' demurrer to the second amended complaint, there is no mandatory duty imposed by any of the statutes cited in the second amended complaint or in Kwik Bonds' papers on this motion that requires Caltrans to ensure the efficacy of a payment bond after the payment bond once a payment bond has been filed that complies with the requirements for such a bond. The undisputed facts establish that Caltrans ensured that a statutorily compliant payment bond was filed as to each of the public work contracts at issue in this case prior to the execution of the contract for each of the public work projects. Having done so, Caltrans discharged the sole mandatory duty imposed on it with regard to the securing of a payment bond as to each public work project. There is no California authority that imposed any further duty on Caltrans such as a duty to ensure the efficacy of the payment bond in light of the financial, legal or other circumstances of the general contractor who filed the bond or the surety who issued the bond. Public Contract Code 10225 does not impose such a duty and, even if it did, that duty would be limited to circumstances that do not obtain here where the surety cancelled or withdrew a payment bond. Because there is no mandatory duty as alleged in the second amended complaint, Caltrans is entitled to judgment in its favor. Counsel for Caltrans is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim all portions of this ruling except for this sentence and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: LENNY SEMIS ET AL VS. ARKADY BERGER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Costs And Attorneys Fees As Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 25, 2016, Line 3, DEFENDANT ALEC BERGER Motion For Costs And Attorneys Fees As Sanctions. Defendant Arkady Berger's motion for costs and attorney's fees as sanctions is denied. The sole legal basis for the sanctions sought is CCP 128.5. Subsection (f) of that statute requires that a party seeking sanctions comply with the procedures set forth in CCP 128.7(c). Mr. Berger did not comply with the procedural requirements in CCP 128.7(c)(1). He failed to serve plaintiffs Lenny Semis and The Bils Family Trust with the notice of motion for sanctions 21 days prior to filing. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: DARNICE LINTON VS. EILEEN CHAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 25, 2016, Line 5, DEFENDANT EILEEN CHAN DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant Ellen Chan's demurrer to both the general negligence and fraud causes of action in the complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to the general negligence cause of action and sustained with twenty days leave to amend. Plaintiff Darnice Linton contends that he discovered Ms. Chan's alleged wrongful conduct on October 24, 2012. Even if proved, that would still mean his general negligence cause of action is time-barred since it is governed by a two year limitations statute. If he can do so in good faith, Mr. Linton is given leave to file a first amended complaint alleging only a fraud cause of action based on alleged wrongful conduct by Ms. Chan that he did not discover and could not reasonably have discovered until three years or less from the date he filed this lawsuit. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for Ms. Chan is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: JOSEPH BARIBEAU VS. HARRY C. THOMAS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Special Motion To Strike And Special Motion To Strike The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 25, 2016, Line 6, PLAINTIFF JOSEPH BARIBEAU Notice Of Special Motion To Strike And Special Motion To Strike The Pleadings. Cross-defendant Joseph Baribeau's anti-SLAPP motion to strike all three causes of action alleged in the cross-complaint filed by Harry Thomas is granted as to all three causes of action. There is good cause to permit the filing of this motion more than sixty days after service because the cross-complaint clearly alleges protected activity as the bases for all three claims, no trial date has been set and only limited discovery has occurred. All three causes of action alleged by Mr. Thomas arise in substantial part from protected activity including "making¿complaints to the San Francisco Department of Building Inspection¿[and] making¿complaints to the Police Department. Accordingly, Mr. Thomas has satisfied his burden under the first prong of the anti-SLAPP analysis. Mr. Thomas has failed to satisfy his burden under the second prong that any of his claims have the requisite minimal merit needed to defeat an anti-SLAPP motion. rulings.Case Title: BAUER'S INTELLIGENT TRANSPORTATION, INC., VS. FADI JOSEPH STEPHAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Notice Of Motion To Compel Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON TUESDAY, OCTOBER 25, 2016, LINE 4 PLAINTIFF SOCIAL TRENDS, INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION'S Motion To Compel Production Of Documents Pro Tem Judge Stephanie Krmpotic, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted as to Demand For Production of Documents, Set One, No. 56, subject to redaction of privileged information. Motion denied as to Demand For Production of Documents, Set One, No. 57. The parties are ordered to meet and confer further regarding the privilege log submitted on behalf of Bauer's Intelligent Transportation, Inc. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to JSKrmpotic@LOWBALL.COM with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/JPT

rulings.Case Title: THOMAS J. PURCELL VS. UNUM GROUP ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Non-Party Kaiser Permanente Roi To Comply With Business Records Subpoena
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 25, 2016, Line 8, DEFENDANT UNUM GROUP, THE PAUL REVERE LIFE INSURANCE COMPANY, GENERAL AMERICAN LIFE INSURANCE COMPANY Motion To Compel Non-Party Kaiser Permanente Roi To Comply With Business Records Subpoena. The Unum Defendants' motion to compel Kaiser to produce psychiatric records is denied. At page 3 of plaintiff his responsive separate statement plaintiff Thomas Purcell states that his "disability stems entirely from his cervical complaints and he is not making a claim for emotional distress damages." This statement clarifies the issues in this case and shows that Mr. Purcell's psychiatric records are not directly relevant. Mr. Purcell is precluded from arguing or presenting evidence at trial that: 1) his disability is based on anything other than his cervical complaints and 2) he should be awarded emotional distress damages and/or has suffered emotional distress as a result of his asserted disability. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: MATTHEW SCHOENBERG VS. 38 DEGREES NORTH LATITUDE BUILDERS, INC., ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 25, 2016, Line 10, DEFENDANT 38 DEGREES NORTH LATITUDE BUILDERS, INC., PAUL BLICKMAN MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION. Defendants' petition to compel arbitration is granted and the case is stayed pending the conclusion of the arbitration. The parties' contract at paragraph 9(c)(3) provides that the parties will arbitrate "a dispute about the amount due or the quality or Completion of the Work." That contract also incorporates General Conditions of the Contract for Construction ("General Conditions"), which has a broad arbitration clause at paragraph 4.6. Plaintiff's complaint implicates the arbitration clause. The declarations submitted by defendants with the moving papers and their supplemental papers sufficiently demonstrate that the parties' agreement involves interstate commerce. The cases relied on by plaintiff are readily distinguishable. Because the FAA applies, plaintiff cannot rely on Business and Professions Code 7191 to avoid arbitration. That provision is preempted because the FAA prohibits courts from invalidating arbitration agreements under state laws applicable only to arbitration provisions. (Basura v. U.S. Home Corp. (2002) 98 Cal.App.4th 1205, 1212.) Whether or not defendants were properly licensed for the work is a matter for the arbitrator to resolve. Loving & Evans v. Blick (1949) 33 Cal.2d 603, 610 is no longer good law. (Templo Calvario Spanish Assembly of God v. Gardner Const. Corp. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 509, 520.) The fact that a trial court confirming/vacating the arbitration award can review the licensure determination de novo (Ahdout v. Hekmatjah (2013) 213 Cal.App.4th 21) does not change the analysis. Plaintiff cannot avoid arbitration by arguing fraud in the inducement. A fraud in the inducement claim can be arbitrated unless the agreement to arbitrate was procured by fraud. There are no facts showing that the agreement to arbitrate was the result of fraud. (Eriksen, Arbuthnot, McCarthy, Kearny & Walsh, Inc. v. 100 Oak Street (1983) 35 Cal.3d 312, 322-323.) Mr. Blickman, a non-signatory, can compel arbitration as defendant's agent and plaintiff fails to show that there is a realistic chance of conflicting rulings to warrant denial of the motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: ROSEMARY MARTIN VS. FORD MOTOR COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Ford Motor Company'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Transfer To Fresno County (Change Of Venue)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 25, 2016, Line 11, Defendant Ford Motor Company's Motion To Transfer To Fresno County (Change Of Venue). (Part 1 of 2). Defendant Ford Motor Company's motion to transfer venue is denied. San Francisco County is a proper venue since Ford has not established a California principal place of business and is therefore subject to venue in any county in California. (Easton v. Superior Court (1970) 12 Cal.App.3d 243, 246). Case law establishes that when a non-California corporation without a principal place of business in California is sued and thus the "any county" venue rule applies, an individual defendant residing in California but not in the county where the lawsuit was filed has the right to change venue to his or her county of residence. (Fitzpatrick v. Sonoma County (1929) 97 Cal. App. 588, 596; see also Brown v. Superior Court (1984) 37 Cal. 3d 477, 483-84, n. 6 ("when a plaintiff brings an action against several defendants, both individual and corporate, in a county in which none of the defendants reside, an individual defendant has the right to change venue to the county of his or her residence.")). Ford argues that, when a California corporation who does not have its principal place of business in the county where the lawsuit is filed is a co-defendant with a non-California corporation that does not have its principal place of business in California, the case must be transferred to the county where the California corporation has its principal place of business. Although there is dicta supporting this argument in the 110 year old case of Ludington Exploration Company v. La Fortuna Gold and Silver Mining Company (1906) 4 Cal. App. 369, 370, no California case so holds nor has any California case ever applied such a rule. Nor is the proposition advanced by Ford mentioned in any of the leading California treatises on venue. Since it is common for a California corporation to be sued along with a non-California corporation, the absence of any case applying the rule advocated by Ford or any treatise referring to such a rule is telling. Ford argues that the "any county" venue rule is an interpretation of the sentence in CCP 395(a) providing that "If none of the defendants reside in the state¿the action may be tried in¿any county" and thus the any county venue rule is inapplicable unless no defendant, either individual or corporate, resides in California. While this argument has superficial appeal and is supported by the dicta in Ludington, no case has ever adopted this position. The better interpretation of that sentence, and the one consistent with the footnote in Brown, is that the word "reside" in the sentence "If none of the defendants reside in the state¿the action may be tried in¿any county" refers only to individual defendants who have a "residence" and excludes corporations who, instead of a residence for purposes of CCP 395(a), have a "principal place of business" for purposes of CCP 395.5. Ford's argument that its motion should be granted based on CCP 395(b) and the interests of justice are improper because they were made for the first time in the reply brief and, in any event, those arguments lack merit. As indicated in Fontaine v. Superior Court (2009) 175 Cal. App. 4th 830, the sole case cited by Ford for its invocation of 395(b), that statute was intended to provide broader rights to a consumer plaintiff, not restrict them, as Ford seeks to do. "Interests of justice" is a standard used for a motion to change venue due to witness convenience, but is inapplicable to a motion asserting that the case was filed in the wrong county. (Continued to Part 2). =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: JAMES D. WARREN VS. JONATHAN DRAKE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: APPLICATION AND HEARING FOR RIGHT TO ATTACH ORDER
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 25, 2016, Line 17, PLAINTIFF JAMES WARREN APPLICATION AND HEARING FOR RIGHT TO ATTACH ORDER. Plaintiff James Warren's application for a writ of attachment and right to attach order as to defendant Scott Larson is denied. California's attachment statutes are strictly construed. (Jordan-Lyon Productions, Ltd. v. Cineplex Odeon Corp. (1994) 29 Cal.App.4th 1459, 1466; Hobbs v. Weiss (1999) 73 Cal.App.4th 76, 79-80.) Per CCP 484.020, an application for writ of attachment must show that the attachment is not sought for a purpose other than recovery. Mr. Warren has not met this requirement. His very recent acquisition of the Bank's rights in the loan agreements including the guaranty agreements, considered in the context of the disagreements between and among ZoMazz's current and former shareholders, directors and officers, strongly suggests that the attachment is sought to gain leverage vis-à-vis the defendants. The declarations of Mr. Larson, Mr. Drake and Mr. Warren show that: a) the parties are embroiled in a corporate power struggle regarding ZoMazz; and b) Mr. Warren purchased and seeks to recover on the Bank's interest in the Zomazz loans and guarantees as a means of leveraging favorable terms in acquiring the defendants' interests in ZoMazz. Such conduct does not meet the purpose requirement of CCP 484.20(c). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order that recites the relief requested by the motion and states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)
